name: Atalanta
description: Atalanta is a prominent figure in Greek mythology, celebrated as a skilled huntress, athlete, and a symbol of female independence and prowess. Unlike many female figures in Greek myths who are often defined by their relationships to men or their roles within the domestic sphere, Atalanta actively rejects traditional gender roles. Abandoned by her father as an infant due to his disappointment that she was not a son, she was raised by a bear (in some accounts) or by hunters. This unconventional upbringing instilled in her a fierce independence and a mastery of the wilderness. She is most famous for her incredible speed and hunting skills, participating in the Calydonian Boar hunt and defeating numerous suitors in a footrace to avoid marriage. Her story explores themes of fate, free will, and the societal expectations placed upon women in ancient Greece, highlighting the complexities of female agency within a patriarchal system. Atalanta’s narrative continues to resonate as a symbol of strength, determination, and defiance against prescribed gender norms.
subtitle: The Swift-Footed Huntress

keyMyths.title: The Calydonian Boar Hunt
keyMyths.description: Atalanta joined the Calydonian boar hunt, a perilous expedition to kill a monstrous boar sent by Artemis to ravage Calydon. Despite initial reluctance from some of the male heroes to hunt alongside a woman, Atalanta proved her worth by being the first to wound the beast. Meleager, the leader of the hunt, ultimately killed the boar but awarded Atalanta the spoils of the hunt as recognition of her contribution. This act sparked conflict amongst the hunters, demonstrating the societal challenges Atalanta faced due to her gender.

keyMyths.title: Atalanta's Footrace
keyMyths.description: To avoid marriage, Atalanta declared she would only marry the man who could defeat her in a footrace. Those who lost would be killed. Many suitors perished until Hippomenes (or Melanion in some versions) sought Aphrodite's help. She gave him three golden apples, which he used to distract Atalanta during the race. Each time Atalanta gained on him, he threw an apple, causing her to stop and pick it up, thus allowing him to win. This myth highlights the cunning often employed to overcome female strength.

keyMyths.title: Transformation into Lions
keyMyths.description: After Hippomenes won the race and married Atalanta, the couple angered either Zeus or Cybele by making love in a sacred space dedicated to them. As punishment, they were transformed into lions. Ancient Greeks believed that lions could not mate with each other, only with leopards, thus the transformation served as a permanent separation and a further punishment for their transgression. This myth reinforces the idea that even the most powerful individuals are subject to divine retribution.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive source of Greek mythology, providing detailed accounts of Atalanta's involvement in various myths, including the Calydonian Boar hunt.
sources.text: A comprehensive source of Greek mythology, providing detailed accounts of Atalanta's involvement in various myths, including the Calydonian Boar hunt.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: A Roman epic poem that recounts the story of Atalanta's footrace and her subsequent marriage to Hippomenes, emphasizing the role of Aphrodite and the golden apples.
sources.text: A Roman epic poem that recounts the story of Atalanta's footrace and her subsequent marriage to Hippomenes, emphasizing the role of Aphrodite and the golden apples.

sources.author: Hyginus
sources.description: A collection of mythological stories that includes the account of Atalanta and Hippomenes' transformation into lions as punishment for their sacrilege.
